title: The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter and evangelion
display_title: The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter and evangelion
wikitext:
[]: The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter and evangelion             Quote Post by orcot » Sat Dec 26, 2020 3:13 am Was The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter one of the sources of inspiration for evangelion? The story involves people finding a divine lady on earth (that we later find out came from the moon) The lady grows up fasther then normal. afther some shenanigans and making friends with the emperor it's time for her to return to the moon. when it's her time to return (to the moon) the emperor send out a army whilst a embassy of heavenly beings decend (from the moon) to retrieve her. Eventually she does need to return. Their seems to be a lot of similarities to the first draft of evangelion I think. (evanglelion was way darker from the start tough) here is the story for those interested https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_Japa ... Moon-Child

[]: Re: The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter and evangelion             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Dec 26, 2020 7:25 am I highly doubt it, apart from being produced in the context of the same cultural "background radiation" This seems like asking if Sartre's "No Exit" is based on the bible or any particular French fairytale that features the concept of heaven/hell of medieval European Christianity. Like there are a lot of anime that reference the story but they would probably invoke the symbolism more directly, like having some analogue to the Five Treasures, or plopping in a powerful individual to stand in for the emperor etc. Vague similarities can be chalked up to similar cultural concepts or even mythic universals (see all the European changeling or spirited away type stories- See all the countless "god hides on earth" or "got takes human form" stories all over the world - demigods, avatars, the whole shebang, plus whatever African or Native American spiritual concepts that my education has neglected to acquaint me with. It would be interesting to discuss in how far East Asian Spiritual/Mythical concepts have made it in (after all, just because they're borrowing a different "dictionary" of Symbols doesn't mean you've unlearned the old or no longer have the echos of the old one present) Things like nirvana, reincarnation, the as the "impure" world while is "pure" I once read an interesting interpretation that compared the Lance of longinus to the Spear of Izanagi (since it is used for world creation - though I get that the aspect lifted from the western myth here is "weapon used against a god" and "mythical artifact") Your existing PoV can certainly be seen in how you interpret a new set of symbols. For example there's the dualist "good vs evil" narrative on "chaos versus order" stories, the search for a "Satan Figure" - perfectly natural to Christianity & Zoastrianism (which are themselves different aside from the one feature of dualism), but very foreign even to non-dualistic monotheism like Islam or Judaism, let alone non-monotheism. In Hesiods Theogony the closest thing to a villain is Gaia. A lot of Anime authors find a lot of resonance with the idea of the Seven Deadly Sins but then it's often filtered through the lens of the Buddhist "Desires are the cause of suffering" lens, & the emphasis is on the desires / basic feelings of envy, lust etc. for example... Whereas if you look at the actual catholic theory, it doesn't care about feelings rather there is an emphasis on action mechanistic explanation of how it is selfish (Greed: "If you take it you keep it from your neighbors") Basically in the influence sphere of confuscianism,Buddhism & the like you find collectivistic morals while cultures influenced by the Roman Empire demand self-sacrifice to the authority Though ultimately all springs from the same human psychology so cultural BS is more alike than similar in most places. Maybe the creative thinkers are different but fundamentalists are the same everywhere. There's a big difference between say, something like Narnia which is written from the PoV of a believer, and an atheist European author using a reference to the Greek or Norse gods as a metaphor because it's in their cultural "dictionary" of symbols. And in both cases, each myth has various interpretations even in mainstream, and then you get individual authors putting new spins on it that might be controversial. (Just all the takes in contemporary fantasy that make demons a symbol of freedom instead of evil. - "Rather rule in hell than serve in heaven" - one could perhaps relate instrumentality to Nirvana in a similar way, though probably more in the second, "sci fi author using greek symbolism as metaphor" way ) So even if some cultural concepts from Japanese myth/culture etc got in here they are likely to be present in a non-standard interpretation

[]: Re: The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter and evangelion             Quote Post by Blockio » Sat Dec 26, 2020 7:11 pm Kendrix summed it up very well, yeah. As soon as something is popular enough, elements of it start passing over into the general narrative conscious and appear in other stories. I'm not familiar enough with the tale of the bamboo cutter to offer any morein-depth insights there, but since the people who are way into seeking out the mythological inspirations for Eva on here and elsewhere have never brought it up and it's not exactly obscure, I'm inclined to agree that it's just correlation
